current_fact.fact_text: On April 14, 1940, Royal Marines landed at Namsos in Norway as part of a hastily organized Allied attempt to block German advances. The operation unfolded among fjords and forests, showing how World War II often hinged on remote, rugged landscapes as much as grand battlefields.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

current_fact.match_reason: A story of marines landing under fire connects with an episode about defiant, amplified courage—different battles, same refusal to ask permission.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1895, the Ljubljana earthquake struck what is now Slovenia, toppling buildings and reshaping the city’s skyline. The disaster led to a wave of reconstruction that helped transform Ljubljana from a provincial town into a modern European capital.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A city rebuilding after an earthquake echoes an episode about healing after sudden damage, and the slow reconstruction of strength.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1894, the first commercial motion picture house opened in New York City, featuring ten Kinetoscopes that showed short films to one viewer at a time. People queued to peer into wooden cabinets, paying to see moving images that lasted only seconds—cinema as a private peep-show before it became a shared darkened-room ritual.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

queue.match_reason: Early kinetoscope booths and a return to an iPod both celebrate intimate, intentional ways of consuming media one experience at a time.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1999, an intense hailstorm hammered Sydney with ice stones the size of tennis balls, damaging thousands of roofs and cars. It became Australia’s costliest natural disaster in insurance terms, a reminder that the sky can suddenly turn into a barrage of falling stone.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A brutal storm of ice over Sydney aligns with an episode about rain, weathering emotional storms, and the sky eventually clearing.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1988, the USS Samuel B. Roberts struck an Iranian mine in the Persian Gulf, ripping a massive hole in its hull. The crew’s desperate damage-control efforts kept the ship from sinking, turning a near-loss into a textbook case of maritime survival.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A warship fighting to stay afloat in the Gulf connects thematically to an episode recorded at sea, meditating on life aboard a boat and staying steady on the water.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1929, the inaugural Monaco Grand Prix roared through Monte Carlo’s narrow streets, with William Grover-Williams winning in a Bugatti Type 35. The race turned a tiny principality into a high-speed street circuit, where engines bounced off harbor walls and hairpin turns.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: A glamorous street race full of motion suits an episode built for being on the move, all engines on, starting fresh at 'Zero Zero'.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1775, the Society for the Relief of Free Negroes Unlawfully Held in Bondage was formed in Philadelphia, the first abolition society in North America. Led by figures like Benjamin Franklin and Benjamin Rush, it challenged a system that many considered unchangeable.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: The first abolition society fought to unbox humans from the label of property, aligning with an episode about breaking out of imposed boxes and confronting injustice.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 2024, severe flooding swept across parts of the Persian Gulf, with Oman particularly hard-hit and at least 19 people killed. Torrents turned usually dry landscapes into temporary rivers, a stark sign of how climate extremes are reshaping familiar regions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: Catastrophic floodwaters and an episode themed around immersion and water make a poignant pairing, contrasting destructive and meditative forms of being submerged.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 2003, researchers announced the completion of the Human Genome Project, with 99% of the human genome sequenced to 99.99% accuracy. For the first time, we had a near-complete map of our own genetic instruction manual, turning biology into something you could read like code.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Decoding the human genome is a monumental learning project, mirroring an episode about curiosity, scientific effort, and how knowledge reshapes us.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1994, two U.S. Air Force fighters accidentally shot down two U.S. Army helicopters over northern Iraq during Operation Provide Comfort, killing 26 people. The tragedy became a haunting case study in how miscommunication and misidentification can turn allies into unintended targets.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A deadly friendly-fire error rooted in communication and perception issues contrasts with an episode about our fraught relationship to technology and signals, asking us to rethink how we connect.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1988, the Soviet Union signed the Geneva Accords, agreeing to withdraw its troops from Afghanistan after nearly a decade of war. The pact marked the beginning of the end for one of the 20th century’s most grueling occupations, often called the USSR’s “Vietnam.”

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A major Cold War withdrawal and the politics of occupation fit the raw, anti-war, anti-imperialist fire of a Rage Against the Machine-focused episode.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1986, the heaviest hailstones ever recorded fell on Bangladesh’s Gopalganj District, with some chunks of ice weighing around one kilogram. The storm killed 92 people, showing how extreme weather can weaponize simple frozen water.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A surreal storm of kilo-sized ice falling from the sky pairs with trip-hop’s moody, cinematic weather—slow, heavy, and quietly catastrophic in tone.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1912, the RMS Titanic struck an iceberg in the North Atlantic, beginning a sinking that would claim more than 1,500 lives. The ship was considered practically unsinkable, yet a glancing blow in the dark turned cutting-edge engineering into a cautionary tale about hubris and nature.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Titanic’s collision with an iceberg in frigid northern waters connects with an episode set in Arctic seas, trading tragedy for awe beneath the same cold waves.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1561, citizens of Nuremberg reported a bizarre celestial event: shapes resembling spheres, cylinders, and crosses seemed to battle across the morning sky. The woodcut that recorded it looks like an early UFO report—or perhaps an astronomical phenomenon filtered through a fearful imagination.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A strange ‘aerial battle’ in the sky and an episode about voyaging toward another planet both lean into cosmic mystery and altered perceptions of the heavens.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1997, 16-year-old Pai Hsiao-yen, daughter of Taiwanese entertainer Pai Bing-bing, was kidnapped on her way to school in a crime that would end in her murder. The case horrified Taiwan, transforming public attitudes toward law enforcement, media sensationalism, and the safety of celebrity families.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: A shocking crime in East Asia paired with an episode about discipline and self-defense in Thailand creates a subtle reflection on vulnerability, bodies, and protection in the region.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 2016, the Kumamoto foreshock shook Japan; on that same date in 1895, Ljubljana’s quake hit Europe—two quakes, decades apart, showing how the planet’s crust holds memory in fault lines. Both disasters triggered not only rebuilding, but also advances in seismic science and urban planning.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: The Earth’s sudden violent shifts contrast with a ‘Slow Planet’ mood, inviting listeners to imagine a cooled, soothed world after the shaking stops.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1958, while Sputnik 2 was falling back to Earth, another revolution in seeing was already underway: from 1894’s peephole films to mid‑20th‑century cinemas, motion pictures had become a global language that allowed humans to time-travel into other lives. Every frame is a tiny recorded now, replayed as a shared dream later.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: The birth of film as an art form parallels a deep-dive into Flamenco’s evolution—both are long-running performance traditions carried by stage, screen, and emotion.

queue.fact_text: On April 14, 1958, the same year Sputnik 2 burned up, humans were starting to realize we could also edit ourselves: DNA’s structure had been published just five years earlier, and the path toward projects like the Human Genome Project was quietly set. The sky was no longer the only frontier; the code inside our cells was becoming a new cosmos.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: Mapping the human genome was a big bang for biology, fittingly matched with Sedna’s own first ‘bomb’ of an episode that launched a universe of sound.

queue.fact_text: On April 14 throughout history, from dust storms and hailstones to quakes and floods, Earth has reminded us that it is not a static rock but a restless organism. Each event on this date is like a heartbeat or shiver, a reminder that our planet is constantly improvising its own unpredictable rhythm beneath our feet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A day threaded with natural upheavals closes with an episode about movement and perspective, inviting a meditative way to travel across a restless planet.

published.fact_text: On April 14, 1958, Sputnik 2 re-entered Earth’s atmosphere after 162 days in orbit, ending the mission of the first spacecraft to carry a living animal into space. A stray Moscow dog named Laika became an unwitting pioneer of space biology, her brief life opening the way for human spaceflight and sparking ethical debates that still echo today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal space milestone fits an episode built around rethinking 'emptiness' as creative space and new orbits.

published.fact_text: On April 14, 2023, the European Space Agency launched JUICE, the Jupiter Icy Moons Explorer, on an eight-year journey to study Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto. Its mission is to search for hidden oceans and investigate whether these frozen moons could harbor conditions for life beneath their icy crusts.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A long, mysterious deep-space voyage pairs with a slow-building, ambient-to-groove night journey that feels interplanetary.

published.fact_text: On April 14, 1981, Space Shuttle Columbia completed STS-1, the first orbital test flight of NASA’s reusable shuttle program. For the first time, a winged spacecraft launched like a rocket and landed like a plane, turning spaceflight into something that looked almost routine.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The technological ‘electric shock’ of the first shuttle flight resonates with an episode powered by high-voltage guitar and propulsion energy.

published.fact_text: On April 14, 2016, a powerful foreshock struck Kumamoto Prefecture in Japan, foreshadowing an even larger earthquake that would follow two days later. The event highlighted how complex seismic sequences can be, with ‘warning quakes’ sometimes nearly as damaging as the main shock.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

published.match_reason: An earthquake’s sudden disruption pairs with a reflection on imperfection, fragility, and accepting what doesn’t go to plan.

published.fact_text: On April 14, 1935, the “Black Sunday” dust storm swept across the Great Plains, turning midday skies as dark as night and carrying millions of tons of topsoil into the air. The storm became an icon of the Dust Bowl era, showing how human mismanagement of land could literally strip the earth bare.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A colossal dust storm and the Dust Bowl’s desertification mirror Tuareg desert blues shaped by sand, exile, and survival under harsh skies.

published.fact_text: On April 14, 1940, Royal Marines landed at Namsos in Norway as part of a hastily organized Allied attempt to block German advances. The operation unfolded among fjords and forests, showing how World War II often hinged on remote, rugged landscapes as much as grand battlefields.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: A story of marines landing under fire connects with an episode about defiant, amplified courage—different battles, same refusal to ask permission.
